summary:
Iron working in Africa (c. 1000–500 BC, with regional variation) represents one of the most significant and complex cases of ancient metallurgical history — African iron smelting developed early, was sophisticated, and may have been independently invented rather than diffused from the Near East or Mediterranean. Radiocarbon dates from iron smelting sites in Central Africa (Rwanda, Burundi, Tanzania) suggest dates as early as 1500–1000 BC, which would make African iron working among the world's earliest, predating widespread Mediterranean iron use.

The evidence is most robust for the period 800–500 BC in West Africa (the [[event:1973|Nok Terracotta Sculptures]] Nok culture of Nigeria included iron smelting) and in the Great Lakes region. Distinctive African furnace designs — particularly the preheated blast furnace technology used by the Haya people of Tanzania — achieved temperatures high enough to produce high-carbon steel, a technically demanding feat. These furnaces used unique bellows and tuyere (air pipe) designs that were locally developed. The spread of iron technology across sub-Saharan Africa was closely associated with the [[event:1164|Bantu Migration Early Phase]] Bantu migrations, as iron-using agricultural communities expanded into previously stone-tool-using territories, enabling forest clearance and intensified farming.
